parts.content:
In a coffee shop, the time it takes to serve a customer can be modelled by a normal distribution with a mean of 1.5 minutes and a standard deviation of 0.4 minutes.

Two customers enter the shop together. They are served one at a time.

Find the probability that the total time taken to serve both customers will be less than 4 minutes.

Clearly state any assumptions you have made.
parts.markscheme:
• Find the mean and variance of the total time $T$: $E(T) = 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$ $\text{Var}(T) = 0.4^2 + 0.4^2 = 0.32$ M1 A1
  • State the distribution of $T$: $T \sim N(3, 0.32)$ A1
  • Attempt to find the probability $P(T < 4)$: Using GDC or standardization $Z = \frac{4 - 3}{\sqrt{0.32}} \approx 1.768$ M1
  • Calculate the probability: $P(T < 4) = 0.961$ ($0.96145\dots$) A1
  • State the assumption made: The service times for the two customers are independent. R1
6 marks total

steps.title: Define the total time
steps.content:
Let $X_1$ be the time taken to serve the first customer and $X_2$ be the time taken to serve the second. 

We are looking for the total time $T = X_1 + X_2$. Since both $X_1$ and $X_2$ follow a normal distribution, their sum $T$ also follows a normal distribution.

highlights.text: total time taken to serve both customers

steps.title: Calculate the total mean
steps.content:
The expected value (mean) of the sum of two random variables is the sum of their individual means:

$$\begin{aligned} E(T) &= E(X_1) + E(X_2) \\ &= 1.5 + 1.5 \\ &= 3 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the total variance
steps.content:
To find the variance of the sum, we add the individual variances. First, find the variance of one customer ($Var = \sigma^2$):

$$Var(X) = 0.4^2 = 0.16$$

Now, sum them to find the variance of $T$:

$$\begin{aligned} Var(T) &= Var(X_1) + Var(X_2) \\ &= 0.16 + 0.16 \\ &= 0.32 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the distribution and assumption
steps.content:
For the variance sum rule to work ($Var(A+B) = Var(A) + Var(B)$), we must **assume that the service times for the two customers are independent**. 

The distribution of the total time is:
$$T \sim N(3, 0.32)$$ 

Note that the standard deviation of $T$ is $\sigma_T = \sqrt{0.32} \approx 0.566$.

highlights.text: Clearly state any assumptions you have made

steps.title: Calculate probability with GDC
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that the total time is less than 4 minutes, $P(T < 4)$. 

Using the Normal Cumulative Density Function (Normal CDF) on your calculator with:
- Lower bound: $-10$ (or $-\infty$)
- Upper bound: $4$
- Mean ($\mu$): $3$
- Standard deviation ($\sigma$): $\sqrt{0.32}$

$$P(T < 4) \approx 0.961$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions, then Normal, then Ncd. Set the Lower bound to a very small number, Upper bound to 4, σ to √(0.32), and μ to 3.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select '2:normalcdf('. Enter the values as follows: lower:-1E99, upper:4, μ:3, σ:√(0.32).

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(3, sqrt(0.32)).cdf(max=4)' into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the normal cumulative probability for the total time.

steps.title: State the assumption
steps.content:
To calculate the total time for two customers, we must assume that the time it takes to serve the first customer does not affect the time it takes to serve the second. In statistical terms, we assume the service times for the two customers are **independent** random variables.

This allows us to sum their means and variances directly.

steps.title: Find the total mean
steps.content:
Let $X_1$ and $X_2$ represent the service times for the two customers, and $T$ represent the total time: $T = X_1 + X_2$.

The mean of the total time is the sum of the individual means:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{E}(T) &= \text{E}(X_1) + \text{E}(X_2) \\ \text{E}(T) &= 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the total variance
steps.content:
Since the times are independent, the variance of the total time is the sum of the individual variances. 

First, find the variance of one customer (the square of the standard deviation):

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = 0.4^2 = 0.16$$

Now, sum them for both customers:

$$\text{Var}(T) = 0.16 + 0.16 = 0.32$$

The standard deviation of the total time, $\sigma_T$, is the square root of this variance:

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{0.32} \approx 0.5657$$

steps.title: Calculate the Z-score
steps.content:
We want to find the probability that the total time is less than 4 minutes ($P(T < 4)$). To use the standard normal distribution, we convert the value $T = 4$ into a Z-score using the formula:

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values for the total time:

$$\begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{4 - 3}{\sqrt{0.32}} \\ Z &= \frac{1}{0.56568...} \\ Z &\approx 1.768 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the probability
steps.content:
Now we find the probability that a standard normal variable is less than $1.768$:

$$P(T < 4) = P(Z < 1.768)$$

Using a GDC or standard normal tables, we find:
$$P(Z < 1.768) \approx 0.96145...$$

Rounding to three significant figures, the probability is **0.961**.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distribution -> Normal -> Ncd. Enter: Lower: -9999, Upper: 1.7677, σ: 1, μ: 0.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'normalcdf('. Enter the following: lower: -1E99, upper: 1.7677, μ: 0, σ: 1.

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(0, 1)' in one line, then use the 'Cumulative Probability' checkbox and set the upper bound to 1.7677.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability using the Normal Cumulative Distribution function.

steps.title: Define the sample mean
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Sample Mean Distribution** approach, we first define the average service time for the two customers. Let $X$ represent the service time for one customer, where $X \sim N(1.5, 0.4^2)$. 

For a sample of $n = 2$ customers, the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is also normally distributed. According to the **Central Limit Theorem** (or rules for linear combinations of normal variables), the parameters for the sample mean distribution are:

- The mean of the sample mean is the same as the population mean: $\mu_{\bar{x}} = \mu = 1.5$
- The standard deviation of the sample mean (often called the standard error) is: $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}$

steps.title: Translate the total time
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that the **total time** ($T$) is less than 4 minutes. We can convert this condition into a condition about the **average time** ($\bar{X}$).

Since $T = X_1 + X_2$, the average time is $\bar{X} = \frac{T}{2}$. 

Therefore, the condition $T < 4$ is equivalent to:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{X} &< \frac{4}{2} \\ \bar{X} &< 2 \end{aligned}$$

We are now looking for $P(\bar{X} < 2)$ where $\bar{X} \sim N\left(1.5, \left(\frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2\right)$.

highlights.text: total time taken to serve both customers will be less than 4 minutes

steps.title: State the required assumption
steps.content:
For the distribution of the sample mean to be valid in this way, we must make a key assumption.

**Assumption:** The service times for the two customers are **independent**. This means the time taken to serve the first customer does not affect the time taken to serve the second customer.

highlights.text: Clearly state any assumptions you have made

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.content:
Now we use a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to find $P(\bar{X} < 2)$. 

We enter the following parameters into the normal cumulative distribution function:
- Lower bound: $-\infty$ (or a very small number like $-10^9$)
- Upper bound: $2$
- Mean ($\mu$): $1.5$
- Standard deviation ($\sigma$): $\frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}$

gdc.instructions: In the Statistics menu, go to Distributions -> Normal -> Ncd. Enter Lower: -1E99, Upper: 2, σ: 0.4/√(2), μ: 1.5.

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 2:normalcdf(. Enter lower: -1E99, upper: 2, μ: 1.5, σ: 0.4/√(2).

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(1.5, 0.4/sqrt(2)).cdf(-infinity, 2)' into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability P(X̄ < 2) with mean 1.5 and standard deviation 0.4/sqrt(2).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:

$$P(\bar{X} < 2) \approx 0.961$$

This means there is approximately a $96.1\%$ chance that the total service time for both customers will be less than 4 minutes.

The manufacturer wants to adjust the machine so that the probability of a jar weighing less than 480 g is 0.001.

parts.content: Find the probability that a single jar weighs less than 480 g, assuming $\mu = 500$ g.

steps.title: Identify the distribution parameters
steps.content:
To find the probability using the direct calculator method, we first need to identify our mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$). 

From the question, we are given:
- Mean weight: $\mu = 500$ g
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = 12$ g

This follows a normal distribution, written as $X \sim N(500, 12^2)$.

highlights.text: mean μ g and standard deviation 12 g

steps.title: Determine the calculation bounds
steps.content:
We want to find the probability that a jar weighs **less than 480 g**. This is represented as $P(X < 480)$.

When using a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC), we need to define the interval:
- **Lower bound**: Since weight cannot be significantly negative, we use a very small number like $-10^{99}$ (often entered as `-1E99` on calculators).
- **Upper bound**: $480$ g.

steps.title: Use the GDC command
steps.content: Now, we use the built-in normal cumulative distribution function on the calculator. You will input the lower bound, upper bound, mean, and standard deviation directly.

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the Statistics menu, then Dist > NORM > Ncd. Enter the lower, upper, σ, and μ values.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR). Select '2:normalcdf('. Enter the values as shown.

desmos.instructions: Use the normaldist function to define the distribution and then check the 'Find Cumulative Probability' box.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability using the normal cumulative distribution function.

steps.title: State the final probability
steps.content:
After performing the calculation on your GDC, you get a result of approximately $0.047790...$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, as required by the IB:

$$P(X < 480) = 0.0478$$

steps.title: Identify the distribution
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Standardization Method**, we first define our random variable $X$ as the weight of a jar of jam. 

From the question, we know that $X$ follows a normal distribution with a mean ($\mu$) of $500$ g and a standard deviation ($\sigma$) of $12$ g. We can write this as:

$$X \sim N(500, 12^2)$$

We want to find the probability that a jar weighs less than $480$ g, written as $P(X < 480)$.

highlights.text: mean $\mu$ g and standard deviation 12 g

steps.title: Calculate the z-score
steps.content:
The next step in the standardization method is to convert our $x$-value ($480$) into a **z-score**. The z-score tells us how many standard deviations $480$ is away from the mean.

Using the standardization formula:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} z &= \frac{480 - 500}{12} \\ z &= \frac{-20}{12} \\ z &\approx -1.667 \end{aligned}$$

This means $480$ g is approximately $1.667$ standard deviations below the mean.

steps.title: Find the probability
steps.content:
Now we need to find the probability that $Z$ is less than $-1.667$. Since the normal distribution is symmetrical, we are looking for the area under the standard normal curve to the left of our z-score:

$$P(X < 480) = P(Z < -1.667)$$

Using a GDC or a standard normal table, we find the cumulative probability.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, then Dist -> Norm -> Ncd. Set Lower to a very small number (like -10), Upper to -1.66666667, σ to 1, and μ to 0.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR). Select '2: normalcdf('. Enter -1E99 for lower, -1.666666667 for upper, 0 for μ, and 1 for σ. Select 'Paste' and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(0, 1).cdf(-infinity, -1.66666667)' in a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability using the standard normal distribution function.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
From the calculator, we get a value of approximately $0.04779$. 

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the probability is:

$$P(X < 480) = 0.0478$$

This means there is about a $4.78\%$ chance that a single jar will weigh less than $480$ g.

parts.content: Determine the new mean $\mu$ required to achieve the manufacturer's goal, keeping the standard deviation at 12 g.
parts.markscheme:
Require $P(X < 480) = 0.001$

$\frac{480 - \mu}{12} = -3.090$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

$\mu = 480 + 3.090 \times 12$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\mu = 517.08 \approx 517$ g <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
First, let's list what we know. The weights of the jam jars follow a normal distribution $X \sim N(\mu, 12^2)$. We want to find the new mean, $\mu$, such that the probability of a jar weighing less than $480$ g is exactly $0.001$.

In mathematical terms, we need to solve for $\mu$ in:
$$P(X < 480) = 0.001$$

This falls under **Topic 4.9: Normal distribution** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: probability of a jar weighing less than 480 g is 0.001

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Since we know the probability (the area under the curve), we use the **Inverse Normal** function on our calculator to find the corresponding $z$-score. This $z$-score represents how many standard deviations $480$ g is away from the mean.

Using a standard normal distribution (where $\text{mean} = 0$ and $\text{sd} = 1$), we find the $z$-value such that the area to the left is $0.001$:
$$z \approx -3.090$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics -> Distributions -> Normal -> Inverse Normal. Set Area/Probability to 0.001, Mean to 0, and SD to 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.001, μ: 0, σ: 1, and Paste.

desmos.instructions: Type `inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.001)`

calculator.description: Calculate the z-score for a left-tail area of 0.001.

steps.title: Apply standardization formula
steps.content:
Now we use the $z$-score formula to link our real-world values ($x$, $\mu$, $\sigma$) to the standardized $z$-value we just found.

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our known values:
$$-3.090 = \frac{480 - \mu}{12}$$

steps.title: Solve for the mean
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for $\mu$:

$$\begin{aligned} -3.090 \times 12 &= 480 - \mu \\ -37.08 &= 480 - \mu \\ \mu &= 480 + 37.08 \\ \mu &= 517.08 \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $\mu = 517$ g.

steps.title: Final Answer and Marks
steps.content:
The new mean required is **$517$ g** (or $517.08$ g for more precision).

**Marking Breakdown:**
*   **M1A1**: For correctly finding the $z$-score and setting up the equation $\frac{480 - \mu}{12} = -3.090$.
*   **M1**: For the algebraic steps to isolate $\mu$.
*   **A1**: For the final correct value of $\mu$.

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
First, let's identify what we know. The weights of the jars follow a normal distribution, $X \sim N(\mu, 12^2)$. We are looking for a new value of $\mu$ such that the probability of a jar weighing less than $480$ g is exactly $0.001$. 

Mathematically, we need to solve:
$$P(X < 480) = 0.001$$

highlights.text: mean \mu g and standard deviation 12 g

steps.title: Set up GDC equation
steps.content:
To solve this using your GDC, we use the normal cumulative distribution function ($\text{normalCDF}$). Since we want the area to the left of $480$, our lower bound is a very small number (like $-10^{99}$) and our upper bound is $480$.

The equation to solve is:
$$\text{normalCDF}(-10^{99}, 480, x, 12) = 0.001$$

Where $x$ represents the unknown mean $\mu$.

gdc.instructions: In the Graph menu, plot Y1 = normalcdf(-1E99, 480, x, 12) and Y2 = 0.001. Adjust your window (Xmin: 450, Xmax: 550, Ymin: 0, Ymax: 0.01) and find the intersection point.

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(x, 12).cdf(-infinity, 480) = 0.001' and look for the x-value of the vertical line that appears.

calculator.description: Solve for the mean using the Numerical Solver or Graphing intersection.

steps.title: Perform calculation
steps.content:
Using the numerical solver on your calculator, you will find the value of $x$ (which is $\mu$) that satisfies the equation. 

$$\mu \approx 517.082...$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to the nearest whole number (or 3 significant figures as per IB standards), we get the required mean.

$$\mu = 517\text{ g}$$

parts.content: Using the sample mean of 502 g, perform a hypothesis test at the $5\%$ significance level to test if the mean weight is 500 g against the alternative that it is greater than 500 g. Include a sketch of the distribution with the critical region shaded.
parts.markscheme:
$H_0: \mu = 500$, $H_1: \mu > 500$ <marks>A1</marks>

Under $H_0$, $\bar{X} \sim N\left(500, \frac{12^2}{36}\right)$, so $SE = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

Test statistic $z = \frac{502 - 500}{2} = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

Critical value for $\alpha = 0.05$ is $z = 1.645$ (or $\bar{x}_c = 503.29$) <marks>A1</marks>

Since $1 < 1.645$, we fail to reject $H_0$. There is insufficient evidence to suggest the mean weight is greater than 500 g. <marks>R1</marks>

**Sketch:**
Normal distribution curve centered at 500, with a vertical line at 503.29 (or $z = 1.645$) and the region to the right shaded. <marks>A1</marks>

![Graph](https://chart-studio.plotly.com/~mlewis_97/16.png)

steps.title: Define the hypotheses
steps.content:
First, we state the null hypothesis ($H_{0}$) and the alternative hypothesis ($H_{1}$). The null hypothesis assumes the status quo (the mean is 500 g), while the alternative hypothesis reflects what we are testing for (the mean is greater than 500 g).

$$\begin{aligned} H_{0}: \mu &= 500 \\ H_{1}: \mu &> 500 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: test if the mean weight is 500 g against the alternative that it is greater than 500 g

steps.title: Sampling distribution parameters
steps.content:
We are looking at the distribution of the sample mean, $\bar{X}$. According to the Central Limit Theorem, the sample mean follows a normal distribution with the same mean as the population, but with a smaller standard deviation called the Standard Error ($SE$).

Given the population standard deviation $\sigma = 12$ and sample size $n = 36$:

$$\begin{aligned} SE &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\ SE &= \frac{12}{\sqrt{36}} = 2 \end{aligned}$$

So, under $H_{0}$, the sampling distribution is $\bar{X} \sim N(500, 2^2)$.

steps.title: Calculate the p-value
steps.content:
The $p$-value is the probability of obtaining a sample mean at least as extreme as the observed one (502 g), assuming the null hypothesis is true. We calculate $P(\bar{X} \geq 502)$ where $\bar{X} \sim N(500, 2^2)$.

Using the $Z$-test function on your calculator:

$$p\text{-value} = P\left(Z > \frac{502 - 500}{2}\right) = P(Z > 1) \approx 0.1587$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select 'Test', then 'Z' and '1-Sample'. Enter 'Data: Variable', 'List: None', '$\mu > 500$', '$\mu_0: 500$', '$\sigma: 12$', '$\bar{x}: 502$', and '$n: 36$'.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT], scroll to [TESTS], and select '1: Z-Test...'. Set 'Inpt' to 'Stats'. Enter $\mu_0: 500$, $\sigma: 12$, $\bar{x}: 502$, and $n: 36$. Select the alternative hypothesis '$\mu > \mu_0$'. Select 'Calculate'.

desmos.instructions: Use the normaldist function to find the probability. Calculate the upper tail probability of a normal distribution with mean 500 and standard deviation 2.

calculator.description: Calculate the p-value using a Z-test or Normal CDF.

steps.title: Statistical decision
steps.content:
We compare our calculated $p$-value to the significance level $\alpha = 0.05$.

$$\begin{aligned} p\text{-value} &= 0.1587 \\ \alpha &= 0.05 \end{aligned}$$

Since $0.1587 > 0.05$, the $p$-value is greater than the significance level. We **fail to reject** the null hypothesis $H_{0}$. 

There is insufficient evidence at the $5\%$ level to suggest that the mean weight of the jars is greater than 500 g.

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Given $\sigma = 12$ and $n = 36$:

$$SE = \frac{12}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2$$

steps.title: Calculate the Standard Error
steps.content:
The distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ follows a normal distribution centered at the population mean $\mu$, but with a smaller spread called the standard error ($SE$). 

Using the formula for the standard deviation of the sample mean:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

parts.content: If the true mean is 503 g, calculate the probability of a Type II error for the test in part (c).
parts.markscheme:
Type II error occurs if $H_0$ is not rejected when $\mu = 503$. 

Critical value for rejection is $\bar{x} > 500 + 1.645(2) = 503.29$ <marks>M1</marks>

Under $\mu = 503$, $\bar{X} \sim N(503, 2^2)$ <marks>A1</marks>

$= P\left(Z < \frac{503.29 - 503}{2}\right) = P(Z < 0.145) = 0.558$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the minimum sample size needed to detect a true mean of 503 g with a power of at least 0.9 in the test from part (c).
parts.markscheme:
Power $= P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid \mu = 503) \ge 0.9$, so $\beta \le 0.1$. <marks>M1</marks>

We require $\bar{x}_c = 500 + 1.645\frac{12}{\sqrt{n}}$ and $\bar{x}_c = 503 - 1.282\frac{12}{\sqrt{n}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

$500 + 1.645\frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} = 503 - 1.282\frac{12}{\sqrt{n}}$

$3 = (1.645 + 1.282) \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

$\sqrt{n} = \frac{12 \times 2.927}{3} = 11.708$ <marks>A1</marks>

$n = 137.07 \implies n = 138$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the statistical goals
steps.content:
To solve this, we first need to understand what **power** represents. The power of a test is the probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis ($H_0$) when the alternative hypothesis ($H_1$) is true.

From previous parts of the question, we are conducting a right-tailed test where:
- $H_0: \mu = 500$
- $H_1: \mu > 500$
- Significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ (implied by the $z$-score $1.645$ used in the markscheme)

We are given that the **Power** $\ge 0.9$. This means the probability of a Type II error, $\beta$, must be:
$$\beta = 1 - \text{Power} \le 0.1$$

steps.title: Expression for the critical value
steps.content:
The critical value, $\bar{x}_c$, is the boundary of our rejection region. 

Under the null hypothesis $H_0: \mu = 500$, we reject $H_0$ if the sample mean is significantly higher than $500$. For a $5\%$ significance level, the $z$-score is $1.645$. Using the distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$, we can write:

$$\bar{x}_c = 500 + 1.645 \left( \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} \right)$$

This is our first expression for the critical value based on the null distribution.

steps.title: Expression from the alternative distribution
steps.content:
Now we look at the alternative scenario where the true mean is $\mu = 503$. 

To have a power of $0.9$, the area to the **right** of the critical value $\bar{x}_c$ under the curve centered at $503$ must be $0.9$. This means the area to the **left** is $0.1$.

The $z$-score for the $10^{\text{th}}$ percentile is approximately $-1.282$. Therefore, the critical value is $1.282$ standard errors *below* the alternative mean:

$$\bar{x}_c = 503 - 1.282 \left( \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} \right)$$

This is our second expression for the same critical value.

steps.title: Formulate the algebraic equation
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same critical value $\bar{x}_c$, we set them equal to each other to solve for $n$:

$$500 + 1.645 \left( \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} \right) = 503 - 1.282 \left( \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} \right)$$

Now, we rearrange the equation to isolate the terms involving $n$ on one side and the constants on the other:

$$1.645 \left( \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} \right) + 1.282 \left( \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} \right) = 503 - 500$$

$$(1.645 + 1.282) \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} = 3$$

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.content:
We can now simplify and solve for $\sqrt{n}$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2.927 \times \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} &= 3 \\ \sqrt{n} &= \frac{12 \times 2.927}{3} \\ \sqrt{n} &= 4 \times 2.927 \\ \sqrt{n} &= 11.708 \end{aligned}$$

Finally, square both sides to find $n$:

$$n = 11.708^2 \approx 137.077$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 11.708^2 and press execute.

ti84.instructions: Enter 11.708 and press the [x²] key, then [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 11.708^2 in a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the square of 11.708 to find the sample size.

steps.title: State the final requirement
steps.content:
Since the sample size $n$ must be an integer and we need a power of *at least* $0.9$, we must round up to the next whole number.

$$n = 138$$

To earn full marks, ensure you show the setup of the two $\bar{x}_c$ expressions and the final rounding step.

steps.title: Define the test parameters
steps.content:
To find the power of the test, we first need to define our null and alternative hypotheses. Based on the manufacturer's target, we test if the mean weight $\mu$ is at least $500\text{ g}$.

- $H_0: \mu = 500$
- $H_1: \mu > 500$

From the provided markscheme, the critical value for this test uses a $z$-score of $1.645$, which corresponds to a $5\%$ significance level (one-tailed). The standard deviation is given as $\sigma = 12$.

steps.title: Establish the critical value
steps.content:
The critical value $\bar{x}_c$ is the value of the sample mean beyond which we reject $H_0$. Using the formula for the distribution of the sample mean:

$$\bar{x} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

For $H_0$ to be rejected at the $5\%$ level:

$$\bar{x}_c = 500 + 1.645 \cdot \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}}$$

steps.title: Define the power function
steps.content:
The **power** of a test is the probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis when the alternative is true. We are given the true mean is $\mu = 503$. 

$$\text{Power} = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid \mu = 503)$$
$$\text{Power} = P(\bar{x} > \bar{x}_c \mid \mu = 503)$$

Substituting our expression for $\bar{x}_c$:

$$\text{Power}(n) = P\left(\bar{x} > 500 + 1.645 \cdot \frac{12}{\sqrt{n}} \mid \bar{x} \sim N\left(503, \frac{12^2}{n}\right)\right)$$

We need to find the minimum $n$ such that $\text{Power}(n) \ge 0.9$.

steps.title: Solve using GDC
steps.content: We can now use the numerical solver or graphing tool on the GDC to find where the power equals $0.9$. We define the power function using the Normal Cumulative Distribution (normalCDF) function, where $x$ represents the unknown sample size $n$.

3. Find where the function value equals 0.9.

2. Define the critical value: c(n) = 500 + 1.645 * s(n).
3. Plot the power function: y = 1 - normaldist(503, s(n)).cdf(c(n)).
4. Plot y = 0.9 and find the intersection.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The GDC solver gives:

$$n \approx 137.07$$

Since the sample size must be an integer and the power must be *at least* $0.9$, we must round up to the nearest whole number to ensure the requirement is met.

$$n = 138$$

steps.title: Find the critical z-value
steps.content:
For a $95\%$ confidence interval, we need the critical value $z$ such that $95\%$ of the area under the standard normal curve is between $-z$ and $+z$. This leaves $2.5\%$ ($0.025$) in each tail.

Using the inverse normal function on your calculator, we look for the $z$-score where the area to the left is $0.975$ (which is $0.95 + 0.025$):

steps.title: Apply the interval formula
steps.content:
We use the formula for a confidence interval for the mean when the population standard deviation is known:

$\text{CI} = \bar{x} \pm z \left( \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \right)$

Substituting our values into the formula:

steps.content:
Now we compute the values for the lower and upper bounds of our interval:

- Margin of Error: $1.960 \times \frac{100}{\sqrt{50}} \approx 27.72$
- Lower Bound: $1200 - 27.718\dots = 1172.28$
- Upper Bound: $1200 + 27.718\dots = 1227.72$

Therefore, the $95\%$ confidence interval is:
$$(1172.28, 1227.72)$$

This means we are $95\%$ confident that the true population mean lifespan falls within this range.

steps.title: Analyze the sample size
steps.content: To determine if our confidence interval is valid when the population distribution is unknown, we first look at the sample size ($n$). The question states that a random sample of **50 bulbs** is tested.

steps.title: Apply Central Limit Theorem
steps.content:
Even though the population distribution is **not known to be normal**, we can use the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**. 

The CLT states that if the sample size is large enough (typically $n \geq 30$), the sampling distribution of the mean will be approximately normal, regardless of the shape of the population distribution.

Since our sample size is $n = 50$, which is greater than 30, the CLT applies here.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Because the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal due to the large sample size, the calculation of the confidence interval remains **valid**. 

In your exam, you earn marks by:
1. Stating the calculation is **valid**.
2. Explaining that the **sample size is large enough** ($n=50$) for the **Central Limit Theorem** to apply.

parts.content: Find the probability that the sample mean length is between 44 cm and 46 cm.
parts.markscheme:
recognizing the distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X} \sim N\left(45, \frac{6^2}{64}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

standard deviation of the mean (standard error) $= \frac{6}{\sqrt{64}} = 0.75$

$P(44 < \bar{X} < 46) = P\left(\frac{44 - 45}{0.75} < Z < \frac{46 - 45}{0.75}\right)$

$= P(-1.333... < Z < 1.333...)$ <marks>A1</marks>

$= 0.818$ <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks]

steps.title: Define the sampling distribution
steps.content:
When we take a sample of size $n$ from a normally distributed population, the sample mean $\bar{X}$ also follows a normal distribution. This is a concept from **Topic 4.15: Central Limit Theorem**. 

The distribution of the sample mean is given by:
$$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

Where:
- $\mu = 45$
- $\sigma = 6$
- $n = 64$

steps.title: Calculate the standard error
steps.content:
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution (often called the standard error) is calculated as:
$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{64}} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$$

steps.title: Use GDC for probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that the sample mean is between 44 and 46 cm:
$$P(44 < \bar{X} < 46)$$

Instead of calculating Z-scores manually, we can use the **normCDF** function on our GDC with the following parameters:
- **Lower bound**: $44$
- **Upper bound**: $46$
- **Mean ($\mu$)**: $45$
- **Standard deviation ($\sigma$)**: $0.75$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, then Distributions -> Normal -> Ncd. Set Lower: 44, Upper: 46, σ: 0.75, μ: 45.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 2:normalcdf(. Enter the bounds and parameters: normalcdf(44, 46, 45, 0.75).

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(45, 0.75).cdf(44, 46)' into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the normal cumulative probability for the sample mean.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The GDC provides the probability directly. Rounding to 3 significant figures as per IB standards, we get:

$$P(44 < \bar{X} < 46) \approx 0.818$$

steps.title: Identify the distribution parameters
steps.content:
First, we identify the parameters of the population distribution from the question. We are told the lengths of the fish follow a normal distribution with a mean of 45 and a standard deviation of 6. We also note the sample size.

*   Population mean ($\mu$) = $45$ cm
*   Population standard deviation ($\sigma$) = $6$ cm
*   Sample size ($n$) = $64$

This question involves the **Central Limit Theorem** (Topic AHL 4.15), which describes the distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$.

steps.title: Calculate the standard error
steps.content:
The distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ has the same mean as the population, but its standard deviation (known as the **standard error**) is smaller. 

We calculate the standard error using the formula:
$$\text{Standard Error} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\bar{x}} &= \frac{6}{\sqrt{64}} \\ &= \frac{6}{8} \\ &= 0.75 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us the distribution: $\bar{X} \sim N(45, 0.75^2)$.

highlights.text: recognizing the distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X} \sim N\left(45, \frac{6^2}{64}\right)

steps.title: Calculate the Z-scores
steps.content:
To use the standardization approach, we transform our sample mean boundaries ($44$ and $46$) into **Z-scores** (standard units). The Z-score formula for a sample mean is:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

Calculating for both boundaries:
$$\begin{aligned} Z_1 &= \frac{44 - 45}{0.75} = \frac{-1}{0.75} \approx -1.333... \\ Z_2 &= \frac{46 - 45}{0.75} = \frac{1}{0.75} \approx 1.333... \end{aligned}$$

We are now looking for the probability $P(-1.333... < Z < 1.333...)$ using the standard normal distribution $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

steps.title: Determine the final probability
steps.content:
Finally, we use a calculator to find the area under the standard normal curve between our two Z-scores.

$$P(-1.333... < Z < 1.333...) \approx 0.817577...$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $0.818$.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select '2:normalcdf('. Enter the values: lower: -1.333333, upper: 1.333333, μ: 0, σ: 1. Highlight Paste and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(0, 1).cdf(-1.333333, 1.333333)' into an empty expression line.

parts.content: Find the minimum sample size needed so that the standard error of the sample mean is less than 0.5 cm.
parts.markscheme:
setting up the inequality for standard error: $\frac{6}{\sqrt{n}} < 0.5$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\sqrt{n} > \frac{6}{0.5}$

$\sqrt{n} > 12 \Rightarrow n > 144$ <marks>A1</marks>

minimum $n = 145$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** $n = 144$ is incorrect as it results in a standard error of exactly 0.5.

[3 marks]

steps.title: Identify the standard error formula
steps.content:
To solve this, we first need to recall the formula for the **standard error** (SE) of the sample mean. The standard error is calculated by dividing the population standard deviation ($\sigma$) by the square root of the sample size ($n$):

$$\text{Standard Error} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

From the question, we are given that the standard deviation is 6 cm.

steps.title: Set up the inequality
steps.content:
We are looking for the minimum sample size $n$ such that the standard error is **less than 0.5 cm**. 

Substituting our known value $\sigma = 6$ into the formula, we can set up the following inequality:

$$\frac{6}{\sqrt{n}} < 0.5$$

highlights.text: standard error of the sample mean is less than 0.5 cm

steps.title: Solve for n algebraically
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the inequality to isolate $n$. 

1. Multiply both sides by $\sqrt{n}$ (since $n$ is a positive sample size, the inequality sign stays the same):
$$6 < 0.5\sqrt{n}$$

2. Divide both sides by 0.5:
$$\frac{6}{0.5} < \sqrt{n} \Rightarrow 12 < \sqrt{n}$$

3. Square both sides to solve for $n$:
$$12^2 < n \Rightarrow 144 < n$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 6 / 0.5 and then calculate 12^2.

ti84.instructions: Type 6 / 0.5 and press ENTER. Then type 12^2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 6 / 0.5 followed by 12^2 in the input lines.

calculator.description: Verify the calculation of 6 divided by 0.5 and the square of 12.

steps.title: Determine the minimum sample size
steps.content:
The inequality $n > 144$ tells us that any sample size larger than 144 will result in a standard error less than 0.5. 

Since a sample size must be a whole number, the smallest integer that satisfies $n > 144$ is **145**.

*Note: If we chose $n = 144$, the standard error would be exactly 0.5, but the question requires it to be strictly less than 0.5.*

steps.title: Identify the standard error formula
steps.content:
To find the minimum sample size, we first need to recall the formula for the **standard error of the sample mean**. 

The standard error is given by:
$$\text{Standard Error} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

From the question, we know the population standard deviation $\sigma = 6$. We are looking for the smallest integer $n$ such that the standard error is less than $0.5$.

highlights.text: standard error of the sample mean is less than 0.5 cm

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We want to find the boundary value where the standard error is exactly $0.5$. We set up the following equation to solve for $n$:

$$\frac{6}{\sqrt{n}} = 0.5$$

Instead of solving this algebraically, we can use the **Numerical Solver** on your graphing calculator to find $n$ quickly.

gdc.instructions: Go to the 'Equation' or 'Solver' menu. Enter the equation 6/√(x) = 0.5. Solve for x.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH], then select 'Solver...' or 'Numeric Solver'. Enter the equation 0 = 6/√(x) - 0.5. Provide an initial guess (e.g., x=100) and press [ALPHA][SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the input bar and look for the x-intercept or the value of x that satisfies the equality.

calculator.description: Solve for n using the numerical solver.

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find the value of $n$ that satisfies the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{6}{\sqrt{n}} &= 0.5 \\ \sqrt{n} &= 12 \\ n &= 144 \end{aligned}$$

This tells us that with a sample size of exactly $144$, the standard error is exactly $0.5$.

steps.title: Determine the minimum integer
steps.content:
The question asks for the standard error to be **less than** $0.5$. 

If we check $n = 144$:
$$\text{Standard Error} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{144}} = \frac{6}{12} = 0.5$$

Since $0.5$ is not *less than* $0.5$, we must choose the next largest integer to decrease the standard error (as $n$ is in the denominator, a larger $n$ results in a smaller error).

Therefore, the minimum sample size is:
$$n = 145$$

steps.title: Identify the standard error formula
steps.content:
To solve this, we first need to recall the formula for the **standard error** ($SE$) of the sample mean. The standard error is calculated by dividing the population standard deviation ($\sigma$) by the square root of the sample size ($n$):

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

From the question, we know that the population standard deviation is $6$ cm.

steps.title: Define functions for the GDC
steps.content:
We want the standard error to be less than $0.5$ cm. To find this graphically, we can define two functions on our Graphing Display Calculator (GDC):

1. Let $y_1$ represent the standard error formula: $$y_1 = \frac{6}{\sqrt{x}}$$
2. Let $y_2$ represent the threshold value: $$y_2 = 0.5$$

Here, $x$ represents the sample size ($n$).

highlights.text: standard error of the sample mean is less than 0.5 cm

steps.title: Find the intersection point
steps.content:
Now, use your GDC to find where these two lines intersect. This point tells us exactly when the standard error equals $0.5$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{6}{\sqrt{x}} &= 0.5 \\ \sqrt{x} &= \frac{6}{0.5} \\ \sqrt{x} &= 12 \\ x &= 144 \end{aligned}$$

The intersection occurs at $x = 144$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Open the Graphing menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = 6 / sqrt(x) and f2(x) = 0.5.
3. Adjust the window to see the intersection (e.g., x: [0, 200], y: [0, 1]).
4. Use the G-Solv or Intersect tool to find the point where the graphs meet.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 6 / √(X) and Y2 = 0.5.
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmax = 200 and Ymax = 1.
4. Press [GRAPH].
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '5: intersect'.
6. Press [ENTER] three times to find the intersection.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 6 / sqrt(x) in the first expression line.
2. Type y = 0.5 in the second expression line.
3. Click on the gray point where the curves intersect to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Graph the standard error function and the threshold to find their intersection.

steps.title: Interpret the minimum integer
steps.content:
The intersection point is $(144, 0.5)$. This means that if the sample size is exactly $144$, the standard error is exactly $0.5$ cm. 

However, the question asks for the standard error to be **less than** $0.5$ cm. As the sample size ($x$) increases, the standard error ($y_1$) decreases. Therefore, we need a sample size larger than $144$.

The next whole number is:
$$n = 145$$

At $n = 145$, the standard error is approximately $0.498$ cm, which satisfies the condition.

steps.title: Define the Central Limit Theorem
steps.content: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is a powerful statistical concept. It states that as long as your sample size is large enough, the distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normal, even if the original population distribution is not normal (e.g., if it were skewed or uniform).

steps.title: State the general requirement
steps.content:
In the IB curriculum, a sample is generally considered "large enough" for the Central Limit Theorem to apply if the sample size $n$ is at least 30.

$$n \geq 30$$

steps.title: Compare with the given sample
steps.content:
From the question, we are told that a sample of 64 fish is taken. We need to check if this meets our requirement:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= 64 \\ 64 &> 30 \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the inequality to see if it evaluates to true.

calculator.description: Check if the sample size 64 is greater than or equal to 30.

steps.title: Conclude the applicability
steps.content: Because the sample size $n = 64$ is greater than 30, the Central Limit Theorem is applicable. This means we can treat the sampling distribution of the mean as approximately normal, regardless of how the fish lengths are actually distributed in the lake.

parts.content: Determine the minimum sample size needed to ensure that the $95 \%$ confidence interval for the mean has a width of at most 0.4 liters per 100 km , using the sample variance.
parts.markscheme:
Width $=2 \times 1.96 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.571}{n}} \leq 0.4$. 
<marks>M1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>

$\sqrt{n} \geq \frac{2 \times 1.96 \times \sqrt{0.571}}{0.4} \approx 7.403, n \geq 55$. 
<marks>A1</marks>

Minimum $n=55 . \quad$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the Central Limit Theorem justifies the normality assumption for the sample mean, even if the population distribution were slightly skewed.
parts.markscheme:
For $n=50>30$, Central Limit Theorem ensures $\bar{X}$ is approximately normal, even for slightly skewed populations.
